---
title: "Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building"
---

::: {.day-header}
# Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
::: {.day-subtitle}
From chatbots to agents: context engineering, CLI tools, and building your own AI agent
:::
::: {.day-time}
1:00--4:00 PM EST
:::
:::

[📊 Open the Day 3 slides ↗](slides/day3.html){.btn .btn-primary target="_blank" role="button"}

## Learning Objectives

By the end of today, you will be able to:

1. Distinguish an AI agent from a chatbot, and describe the agent loop and architecture
2. Apply context-engineering principles to design effective agent interactions
3. Explain the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and how tools extend a model
4. Configure agents with Skills (`SKILL.md`) and instruction files (`CLAUDE.md`)
5. Use repeatable "loops" with clear stop conditions to get reliable results
6. Describe multi-agent patterns and when to use them
7. Evaluate agents and set guardrails appropriate for sensitive urban-health work

---

## Software Setup (Windows & macOS) {#setup}

Install these **before class** if you can. Each tool below has a **Windows** and a **macOS** tab --- follow the one for your computer. None of this is needed just to use the web chat tools (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Google AI Studio) --- those only need a browser and a free account.

:::{.callout-tip}
## What to install for what
- **Everyone:** [Positron](#setup) (the IDE), plus **R** and/or **Python** for the data activities.
- **Day 3 CLI demo:** [Node.js + Claude Code](#setup).
- **Local models (Day 3):** [Ollama](#setup).
- **Locked-down work computer?** You can do almost everything in the browser (Claude.ai, ChatGPT, Google AI Studio). Ask your IT department before installing, and see "For IT-Restricted Computers" under Activity 5.
:::

### Positron (the data-science IDE)

Free IDE for R and Python from Posit (the RStudio team). Requires **macOS 12+** or **Windows 10/11**.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
1. Go to the [Positron download page](https://positron.posit.co/download.html) and accept the license.
2. Download the **user-level installer** for your chip --- `x64` for most PCs, `arm64` for Windows-on-ARM.
3. Run the `.exe` and follow the prompts.
4. If it won't launch, install the latest [Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/windows/latest-supported-vc-redist).

#### macOS
1. Go to the [Positron download page](https://positron.posit.co/download.html) and accept the license.
2. Download the `.dmg` for your chip: **Apple Silicon (arm64)** for M1/M2/M3/M4, or **Intel (x64)**. To check, open the Apple menu → About This Mac, or run `uname -m` (`arm64` vs `x86_64`).
3. Open the `.dmg` and drag **Positron** into Applications.
:::

### R

Install from CRAN (not via conda). Positron needs **R 4.2 or higher**.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
1. Download from [CRAN → base](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/) and run the installer.
2. *Optional:* install [Rtools](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/) if you need to compile packages from source.

#### macOS
1. Download the `.pkg` from [CRAN → macOS](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/): **arm64** for Apple Silicon, **x86_64** for Intel.
2. Run the installer (it installs to `/Library/Frameworks/R.framework`).
:::

### Python

Positron works with Python **3.9--3.13**. Easiest path: let **uv** (next section) install Python for you. Otherwise use the official installer.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
1. Download from [python.org/downloads](https://www.python.org/downloads/windows/).
2. In the installer, **check "Add python.exe to PATH"** before clicking Install --- this matters.

#### macOS
1. Download the macOS `universal2` installer from [python.org/downloads](https://www.python.org/downloads/macos/), **or** install via Homebrew: `brew install python`.
:::

### uv (recommended: manage Python versions + project environments)

[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) is a fast tool that creates an isolated environment per project (so packages never collide) and can even install Python for you.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
Open **PowerShell** and run:
```powershell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"
```
Then (optional) let uv supply Python: `uv python install 3.12`

#### macOS
In **Terminal**, run either:
```zsh
brew install uv
# or:
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```
Then (optional) let uv supply Python: `uv python install 3.12`
:::

Per-project use (same on both): `cd` into a folder, then `uv init`, `uv add pandas numpy matplotlib jupyterlab ipykernel`, and run with `uv run python script.py`.

### Node.js + Claude Code (for the CLI demo)

Claude Code is a command-line tool; it needs **Node.js v18+**.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
1. Install the **LTS** build from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org) (`.msi`).
2. Open **PowerShell** (or Windows Terminal) and run:
   ```powershell
   npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
   claude
   ```

#### macOS
1. Install Node from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org) (`.pkg`), or `brew install node`.
2. In **Terminal**, run:
   ```zsh
   npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
   claude
   ```
:::

On first run, `claude` opens a browser to sign in (works with a Claude Pro/Max or Team subscription, or an API key).

### Ollama (run AI models locally)

Runs open models on your own machine --- free and private. After installing, pull a model that fits your laptop (see the [model sizing guide](resources.qmd#which-model-fits-your-laptop)).

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows
1. Download the installer from [ollama.com/download/windows](https://ollama.com/download/windows) and run it.
2. In PowerShell: `ollama pull gemma4:12b` (smaller laptops) or `ollama pull qwen3`.

#### macOS
1. Download the `.dmg` from [ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), or `brew install ollama`.
2. In Terminal: `ollama pull gemma4:12b` or `ollama pull qwen3`.
:::

### No installation needed (browser only)

These need only a browser and a free account: [Claude](https://claude.ai), [ChatGPT](https://chat.openai.com), [Gemini](https://gemini.google.com), [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com), [Perplexity](https://perplexity.ai), and [MakeSense.ai](https://www.makesense.ai/) (Day 2 image annotation).

::: {.callout-warning}
## Skip Anaconda for this course
If you've used Anaconda, **don't rely on its bundled R or Python here** --- it tends to shadow the versions above and cause "wrong version" headaches in Positron. Use **CRAN R** and **uv/python.org Python** as described. If you only want conda for Python, [Miniforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge) is the lighter, license-clean option.
:::

---

## Lecture: Agents, Context, Tools & Multi-Agent Systems

### Chatbots vs. Agents

The AI landscape is rapidly moving from **chatbots** (reactive, single-turn, limited to text) to **agents** (proactive, multi-step, tool-using):

| Feature | Chatbot | Agent |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Interaction | Single turn Q&A | Multi-step task completion |
| Tools | None (text only) | Can use tools (search, code, APIs) |
| Memory | Limited to conversation | Can maintain context across tasks |
| Autonomy | Responds only when asked | Can take initiative, plan, and execute |
| Error handling | Stops at confusion | Can retry, adapt, self-correct |

**The working definition:** an agent is a language model that can use tools in a loop until it completes a task. The agent loop runs: **think/plan → act (use a tool) → observe the result → decide: done or loop again**.

**Why this matters for public health:** Agents can do things like "pull the latest flu surveillance data, compare it to last year, draft a summary report, and email it to the team"---tasks that involve multiple steps, tools, and decision points.

### Agent Architecture: The Five Components

Every agent system, regardless of the harness it runs in, is built from the same five components:

1. **Backbone model:** The LLM that reasons about the task (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.)
2. **Planning module:** How the agent breaks a goal into steps and sequences them
3. **Tool interface:** The mechanism for calling external tools (function calling, MCP)
4. **Memory:** The context window for short-term state, plus any long-term storage
5. **Sandbox / environment:** Where code runs and actions execute safely

An **orchestration harness** (the agent loop) coordinates all five: the model reasons, the planner sequences steps, tools execute, memory preserves state, and the sandbox keeps execution safe. When something goes wrong, one of these five is usually the culprit. Diagnosing agent failures means asking: did the model misunderstand? did the plan go off-track? did a tool fail? did context get lost? did the sandbox block something?

*Adapted from OpenAI, ["A Practical Guide to Building Agents"](https://cdn.openai.com/business-guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-agents.pdf) (2025).*

### Context Engineering: The 5 Components

Context engineering is the practice of designing the information environment that an AI system operates within. As work moves from one-shot chats to agents running over many turns, the question changes from "what do I *say*?" to "what *configuration of context* makes the right behavior likely?" The five key components:

1. **System prompt / Instructions:** What the AI should do, its role, its constraints
2. **User input:** The specific request or query
3. **Retrieved knowledge:** Relevant documents, data, or information pulled in for the task (RAG --- Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
4. **Tool definitions:** What tools the AI can use and how to use them
5. **Conversation history / Memory:** What has happened before in the interaction

Context is a **finite resource** --- every token competes for the model's attention. Managing this state well (compacting, summarizing, pruning) is most of what separates a flaky demo from a reliable agent.

:::{.callout-tip}
## Think of It Like Briefing a New Employee
Context engineering is like preparing a briefing packet for a highly skilled new employee on their first day. You would not just say "do your job." You would provide: their role description (system prompt), the specific task (user input), relevant background materials (retrieved knowledge), access to the tools and systems they need (tool definitions), and notes from previous meetings (conversation history).
:::

### Model Context Protocol (MCP)

MCP is an open standard (created by Anthropic) that allows AI models to connect with external tools and data sources through a standardized interface. Think of it as a "USB port for AI"---a universal way to plug in capabilities. It is now broadly adopted across the major AI providers, with a large ecosystem of ready-made "servers" (connectors).

The architecture is straightforward: **Model ↔ MCP client (the app) ↔ MCP server (the connector) ↔ your tool/data**. A request flows model → client → server → tool, and the result flows back along the same chain.

Examples of MCP tools: file system access (read/write documents), database queries, web search, calendar and email integration, code execution, and API calls to external services.

### Agent Skills

A **Skill** is a reusable folder of expertise you hand the agent. At minimum it is a `SKILL.md` file --- a Markdown document with YAML frontmatter (a `name` and a `description`) and instructions in the body --- plus optional `scripts/` (code the agent can run) and `references/` (documents loaded only when needed). When a task matches the description, the agent loads the skill on demand and follows it.

If MCP is *how* an agent reaches tools, a Skill is *how it knows your way of doing a task*. Where a prompt is a one-off instruction, a Skill is durable and reusable: write your de-identification checklist, your report format, or your STROBE/CONSORT reporting steps once, and the agent applies them every time --- across chats, Claude Code, and Cowork.

You can write a `SKILL.md` by hand, or use the **Skill Creator** (a "skill that builds skills") --- it's pre-installed in Claude Desktop and Cowork, and installable in Claude Code (`/plugin install skill-creator`). On claude.ai, upload a zipped skill folder under **Settings → Customize → Skills**.

*Public-health skill ideas:* a "de-identify before sharing" checklist, a plain-language health-communication rewriter, a grant Specific-Aims formatter, or a data-dictionary generator.

### Configuring Agent Behavior: CLAUDE.md and System Prompts

A `CLAUDE.md` file is a Markdown file in your project root that the agent reads on every launch. Use it for persistent instructions ("always use Python 3.12"), project conventions ("we use snake_case"), domain context ("this repo handles HIPAA data"), and recurring reminders ("run tests before committing"). Claude Code and Cowork read these automatically.

For API-based agents and tools like Google AI Studio, the **system prompt** is the equivalent --- persistent instructions the model sees before every user message. Same purpose, different delivery mechanism. Think of it as the agent's standing orders.

The more specific and domain-aware your configuration, the less you need to repeat yourself --- and the fewer mistakes the agent makes.

### Loops: Repeatable Workflows with Stop Conditions

A "loop" is a reusable instruction that tells an agent what to do, how to check its work, and when to stop --- turning a one-off prompt into a dependable routine.

Examples: a docs sweep that reviews the codebase, updates stale docs, verifies, and stops when docs match the code; a refactor loop that refactors, tests, auto-reviews, and commits after each step; an optimization loop that keeps tuning until a target metric is hit. Each has an explicit **VERIFY** step and a **STOP** condition.

### Build the Loop, Not the Agent

"You are not building an agent. You are building the loop that rebuilds it." --- Forward Future (2025). Three principles:

1. **Fund the iteration:** The artifact (the report, the code, the analysis) is a side effect. The durable asset is the loop that produces it: plan → execute → evaluate → iterate. Invest in making the loop fast and cheap to run.
2. **Expect to rebuild:** Models change, tools change, requirements change. An agent built around a brittle, one-time prompt will break. A loop with explicit evaluation criteria can adapt --- swap the model, swap the tools, keep the loop.
3. **Evaluation is the loop:** Without a way to judge whether the output is good enough, there is no loop --- just a one-shot generation. The VERIFY step is what closes the circle.

*Adapted from Forward Future, ["Build the Loop, Not the Agent"](https://www.forwardfuture.ai/p/build-the-loop-not-the-agent) (2025).*

### Multi-Agent Systems and Orchestration Patterns

When a task is too complex for one agent, split it across several --- each with its own tools, instructions, and expertise. Four common patterns:

- **Manager pattern:** One "boss" agent delegates to specialist sub-agents, reviews their work, and synthesizes the final output. Example: a lead analyst that calls a data agent, a writing agent, and a fact-checker.
- **Sequential handoff:** Agents hand off in a chain. Agent A finishes, passes output to Agent B, and so on. Example: extract → clean → analyze → report.
- **Parallel:** Multiple agents work on different parts simultaneously. A coordinator merges results. Example: three agents each reviewing a different section of a grant.
- **Decentralized:** Agents communicate peer-to-peer with no central coordinator. Google's A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol targets this pattern --- still early.

Start with a single agent. Add agents only when you can clearly articulate what each one does that the others cannot.

*Adapted from OpenAI, ["A Practical Guide to Building Agents"](https://cdn.openai.com/business-guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-agents.pdf) (2025).*

### Agentic Harnesses: Cowork, Claude Code, Codex, and More

The same core idea --- a model plus tools running in a loop --- ships in several "harnesses" that differ mainly in *where* you work and *how much autonomy* they take. (Recall the three ways to work with AI from Day 1.)

| Harness | What it is | Best for | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Claude Code** (Anthropic) | Terminal/CLI agent | Coding, data analysis, file automation in a repo | Reads `CLAUDE.md`; strong on coding benchmarks; Anthropic models |
| **Cowork** (Anthropic) | Desktop app agent | Non-coders: files, connectors, **Skills**, **scheduled tasks** | Runs each task as its own session; paid plan |
| **Codex / Codex CLI** (OpenAI) | Terminal agent | Long, "walk-away" autonomous runs ("goal mode") | Strong Docker sandboxing; OpenAI models |
| **Antigravity** (Google) | Agent-first IDE + CLI/SDK | Orchestrating *multiple* agents; browser-testing what it builds | Free public preview; Gemini models (Gemini CLI folded into it) |
| **OpenCoworker** | Open-source desktop agent | Privacy-sensitive work; bring-your-own model | Free/self-hostable; use your API key or a local Ollama model |

The differences that matter for choosing: **surface** (terminal vs. desktop vs. IDE), **autonomy** (one step vs. hours of unattended work), **model lock-in** (vendor-only vs. bring-your-own), and **data control** (cloud vs. fully local). For sensitive public-health data, weigh the last two heavily.

### Computer Use Agents

A new category of agents can control a mouse and keyboard --- navigating apps and websites the way a human would. **Claude Computer Use** (Anthropic) sees screenshots and emits mouse/keyboard actions, working in any app. **OpenAI Operator** (CUA --- Computer-Using Agent) navigates web apps, fills forms, and completes multi-step web tasks. **Google Mariner** is a browser agent built on Gemini. These are early-stage but evolving fast. Main use cases: automating repetitive UI tasks, accessibility, and connecting to tools that have no API. Always supervise --- they click real buttons.

### Scheduled Tasks

Agentic tools increasingly run on a **schedule**, not just on demand. In Cowork, you describe a task once, pick a cadence (hourly, daily, weekdays, weekly) or an event trigger (a new file, a webhook), and it runs automatically --- each run with access to your files, connectors, and Skills. This turns a one-off ("summarize this week's 311 complaints") into standing automation ("every Monday, pull the new data, update the chart, and draft the email"). Schedule only work you'd be comfortable having run with a human reviewing the output --- not irreversible actions.

### How People Actually Work with Agents

Anthropic studied thousands of real Claude Code sessions and found a consistent pattern: **70% of planning decisions are made by humans**, while **80% of code execution is done by the agent**. Domain expertise matters more than coding skill --- the bottleneck is knowing *what* to build, not *how* to build it. Nine distinct work modes were identified, from quick lookups to deep architecture sessions.

What this means for you: you do not need to be a programmer to direct a coding agent effectively. Public health domain expertise --- understanding your data, your workflows, your constraints --- is more valuable than coding proficiency when working with these tools.

*Source: Anthropic, ["What we learned from studying how people use Claude Code"](https://www.anthropic.com/research/claude-code-expertise) (Jun 2025).*

### Evaluation: Beyond Vibes

An agent that shines in a demo can fail "in the wild." Reliability needs real evaluation, not impressions. Three approaches: work out the **ground truth** (correct answers by hand, then compare), use **LLM-as-judge** (a model grades outputs at scale, calibrated against your ground truth), and **test repeatedly** (track where and how it fails across many cases, not just whether one demo worked). Especially important when wrong answers have real consequences.

*Adapted from Nesta, ["Beyond Vibes: How to Actually Evaluate AI Agents"](https://www.nesta.org.uk/report/beyond-vibes/) (2025).*

### Guardrails for Agentic Work

- **Human in the loop:** Approve consequential or irreversible actions
- **Least privilege:** Give only the access the task needs; sandbox execution
- **Verify outputs:** Check facts, code, and numbers before acting on them
- **Protect data:** Keep protected health data out of tools that aren't approved for it

Agents act in the world --- so the stakes of a mistake are higher than with a chatbot. Scope and supervise accordingly.

### Verification Is the Bottleneck

Agents generate far faster than you can check, so verification --- not generation --- becomes the limiting step and the real skill. Fluent output is not the same as correct output, and the higher the stakes, the higher your burden of proof. One of the most reliable uses of a model is to turn it on its own work: ask it to be an *adversarial reader* ("argue why this analysis is wrong"), to find the weakest claim, the missing control, or the unchecked assumption in your code, draft, or study design --- then you decide.

*Adapted from Beam & Aslim, ["Thinking With Agents"](https://thinkingwithagents.github.io/) (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).*

---

## Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise {.activity}

::: {.activity}

### Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise
::: {.activity-meta}
Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Small group design exercise
:::

**Goal:** Design a context package for a specialized public health AI assistant.

#### Scenario

You are designing a **"Neighborhood Health Assessment AI Assistant"** for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. This agent should be able to:

- Pull demographic and health data for any Philadelphia neighborhood
- Compare indicators across neighborhoods
- Generate assessment reports with visualizations
- Identify health equity concerns
- Recommend evidence-based interventions

#### Your Task

In your small group, design the **five context components** for this agent:

**1. System Prompt / Instructions**

- What role should the agent assume?
- What are its core capabilities?
- What constraints should it have? (e.g., never provide individual-level data, always cite sources, flag uncertainty)

**2. User Input Examples**

- Write 3 example queries a health department analyst might ask this agent

**3. Retrieved Knowledge**

- What documents, datasets, or knowledge bases should this agent have access to?
- How often should they be updated?

**4. Tool Definitions**

- What tools should this agent be able to use? (e.g., database query, mapping, chart generation, report writing)

**5. Conversation History / Memory**

- What should the agent remember across conversations?
- What should it forget (for privacy/security)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: a filled-in context package

A model answer for the Neighborhood Health Assessment AI Assistant. Use it to check your own design — it is *a* good answer, not the only one.

**1. System Prompt / Instructions**

> You are a Neighborhood Health Assessment Assistant for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. You help analysts compare health and demographic indicators across Philadelphia neighborhoods and draft assessment summaries.
>
> Rules: Report data only at the census-tract or neighborhood level — never individual records. Cite the dataset and year for every number. When an indicator rests on a small sample or wide confidence interval, say so. If asked for a recommendation, ground it in the cited evidence and label it a suggestion for human review. If data is missing or you are unsure, say "I don't have that" rather than estimating.

**2. User Input Examples**

1. "Compare heat vulnerability and tree canopy for Hunting Park vs. Chestnut Hill, and tell me which has greater need."
2. "Which 10 tracts in North Philadelphia have the highest asthma ED-visit rates, and what built-environment factors correlate?"
3. "Draft a one-page heat-vulnerability summary for Hunting Park for a community meeting."

**3. Retrieved Knowledge**

- OpenDataPhilly: Heat Vulnerability Index (2023), tree canopy, 311 service requests
- American Community Survey tract tables (income, age, language, vehicle access) — refresh annually
- PDPH community health assessment indicators; asthma and heat-related ED-visit summaries
- A data dictionary defining each indicator with its source and year

**4. Tool Definitions**

- `query_indicator(area, indicator, year)` → returns value + source + confidence interval
- `make_map(indicator, area)` → choropleth map
- `make_chart(indicators, areas)` → comparison bar/line chart
- `write_brief(findings, audience)` → formatted summary draft

**5. Conversation History / Memory**

- *Remember:* the analyst's current focus area and preferred output format, within a session.
- *Forget:* anything containing identifiable data; clear the focus area at session end; never persist queried data beyond the session.
:::

#### Share Out

Each group presents their context design in 2 minutes. Class discusses: What did groups prioritize differently? What components were hardest to define?

:::

---

## Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough {.activity}

::: {.activity}

### Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough
::: {.activity-meta}
Duration: ~25 minutes | Format: Instructor demonstration (follow along if you have Claude Code installed)
:::

**Goal:** See how a command-line AI tool can perform data analysis, write code, and generate documents directly on your computer.

#### What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is a command-line interface (CLI) tool from Anthropic that lets you interact with Claude directly from your terminal. Unlike the web-based chat, Claude Code can:

- Read and write files on your computer
- Execute code and see results
- Navigate your file system
- Chain multiple operations together
- Work with local data that never leaves your machine (though prompts are still sent to Anthropic's servers)

#### Demo Outline

The instructor will demonstrate:

**1. Data Analysis and Visualization**

```
claude "Load the Philadelphia HVI dataset from data/philadelphia_hvi.csv.
       Summarize it and generate a bar chart of the 10 census
       tracts with the highest hvi_score. Save the chart
       as hvi_top10.png"
```

**2. Document Generation**

```
claude "Read the analysis results in output/ and draft a
       2-page executive summary for city council. Include
       the key findings, a table of the most vulnerable
       tracts, and three policy recommendations.
       Save as executive_summary.docx"
```

**3. Multi-Step Workflow**

```
claude "Download the latest flu surveillance data from CDC,
       compare Philadelphia's rates to the national average,
       create a trend visualization, and draft a weekly
       update email for the department director"
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: what good output looks like (and a planted error to catch)

After **Step 1**, Claude's data summary should read roughly like:

> 384 records, one per Philadelphia census tract (2023). Columns include `name10` (tract ID) and `hvi_score` (0–4) plus component indicators. No missing `hvi_score` values; two tracts have null population — likely parks or non-residential land.

After **Step 3**, a good `brief.md` might open:

> **Heat Vulnerability in Philadelphia — Council Brief.** Of 384 tracts, the five most heat-vulnerable (HVI = 4) sit in North and lower Northeast Philadelphia. They combine high surface temperature, low tree canopy, and larger shares of older adults living alone…

**The teaching moment:** ask Claude for "the average `hvi_score` across all tracts," then verify it yourself — open the CSV in Positron and run `mean(hvi_score)`. If Claude's prose implies "most tracts are highly vulnerable" but the mean is ≈2.0, that gap between fluent text and the actual numbers *is* the lesson. Generation is cheap; checking is the job.
:::

#### Key Takeaways

- CLI tools are powerful for **integrating AI into existing workflows**
- They work with your local files and tools
- They enable **automation** of repetitive analysis-report-communicate cycles
- They require some comfort with the command line (but AI can help with that too)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Instructor Walkthrough: Running the Claude Code Demo Live

This is a step-by-step guide for the instructor to run the demo end-to-end on a real dataset. It uses a small **sample** Heat Vulnerability dataset so every command actually executes. Total live time: ~20--25 minutes. (Verified against Claude Code documentation, 2026; the CLI changes often, so test once the day before.)

### Before class (15 minutes, once)

1. **Install** (needs [Node.js](https://nodejs.org) v18+; check with `node --version`):

   ```bash
   npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
   claude --version          # confirm it installed
   ```

   Do *not* use `sudo`. If you hit permission errors, use `nvm` or reconfigure npm's global prefix.

2. **Authenticate.** Run `claude` once; a browser opens to approve login. This works with a Claude Pro/Max **or** Team/Enterprise subscription (recommended for teaching), or with an API key from the [Console](https://console.anthropic.com).

   > **Cost gotcha:** if `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is set in your shell, it overrides your subscription and you'll be billed per-token. For a subscription demo, `unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` first.

3. **Build the demo folder** and drop in the data ([download `philadelphia_hvi.csv`](data/philadelphia_hvi.csv){download="philadelphia_hvi.csv"} --- the real OpenDataPhilly HVI: 384 Philadelphia tracts, 2023):

   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/cli-demo/data && cd ~/cli-demo
   # move the downloaded file into ~/cli-demo/data/philadelphia_hvi.csv
   ```

4. **Dry-run it once** so there are no surprises live, then `/clear` before class.

### Live demo (project on screen, narrate as you go)

**Step 0 --- Start in the demo folder (1 min).** Open the terminal in `~/cli-demo` and launch the REPL:

```bash
cd ~/cli-demo
claude
```

Point out the prompt is now waiting, and that Claude can see this folder. Type `/help` to show the slash commands, then `/cost` to show that usage is tracked.

**Step 1 --- Explore the data (read-only; ~4 min).** Paste:

> Look at data/philadelphia_hvi.csv. How many records are there, what is the unit of analysis, what columns are present, and are there any data-quality issues? Don't write any files yet --- just summarize.

*What to narrate:* Claude reads the file without asking permission (reads are allowed by default), but it has **not** changed anything. This is the safe way to start: explore before you act. Tie back to context engineering (Activity 1) --- it only knows what's in the folder plus your instructions.

**Step 2 --- Generate a chart (first permission prompt; ~6 min).** Paste:

> Create a horizontal bar chart of the 10 tracts with the highest hvi_score, labeled by name10 (the tract number). Use Python (matplotlib), save it as figures/hvi_top10.png, and create the figures/ folder if needed.

*What to narrate:* Claude now wants to **write a script and run it**, so it pauses and shows you the command and the file it will create. **Approve it in front of the class** --- this is the human-in-the-loop checkpoint and the single most important thing to show. Open `figures/hvi_top10.png` afterward.

**Step 3 --- Draft a brief from the findings (~5 min).** Paste:

> Using what you found, draft a one-page plain-language brief for City Council on heat vulnerability, with the top 5 tracts (by name10) in a table and three policy recommendations. Save it as brief.md.

*What to narrate:* Same approval step for the new file. Then **open `brief.md` and read a recommendation aloud, then push back**: ask the class "is that number actually in our data?" Open the CSV and check. This is the *verification-is-the-bottleneck* point from earlier --- fluent output still needs checking, especially before anything reaches a decision-maker.

**Step 4 --- Show the controls (~3 min).**

- `/permissions` --- how to pre-approve or block specific commands.
- Press **Shift+Tab** to cycle to **plan mode** (read-only): Claude proposes a plan but cannot edit. Good for "think before you touch my files."
- `/clear` to wipe context; `/cost` to show total session cost; `/exit` (or Ctrl+D) to quit.

### Alternative: run it inside Positron (recommended for R/Python users)

Claude Code is still the CLI here --- you just host it in [Positron](https://positron.posit.co) (Posit's R/Python IDE) instead of a bare terminal. For a data-science audience this is usually the better venue: students stay in a familiar environment, and Claude's edits and outputs appear right in the IDE.

1. **Open the demo folder in Positron:** *File &rarr; Open Folder &rarr; `~/cli-demo`.*
2. **Open the integrated terminal** (*Terminal &rarr; New Terminal*, or ``Ctrl+` ``) and run `claude` there. Claude Code auto-detects the IDE (via a lock file in `~/.claude/ide/`), so it knows which file you have open and shows its edits as **diffs in the editor** --- which is much clearer to project than a terminal diff.
3. **Keep it in your language.** Add to your prompts: *"Use R and ggplot2; save the script as a .R file and run it with Rscript"* (or *"use Python"*). Run the same four steps as above.
4. **Verify in the IDE.** Open `philadelphia_hvi.csv` in Positron's **data viewer**, let the chart land in the **Plots** pane, and run one line of R/Python yourself to confirm Claude's numbers --- a clean, visible version of the verification step.

> **Caveat:** Claude runs commands in that terminal's shell, so the R/Python it uses is whatever is on your **PATH**, not necessarily Positron's selected interpreter. Confirm `Rscript` (or your Python environment) works in that terminal *before* class.

Positron also ships a built-in **Positron Assistant** (a native in-IDE chat/agent, bring-your-own Claude API key). That's a different flavor from the agentic-CLI lesson, so keep Claude Code as the tool you demo --- but it's worth mentioning to students as another option.

### If something breaks (fallbacks)

- **Can't install / locked-down laptop:** run the *same prompts* against [claude.ai](https://claude.ai) or [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com) by uploading `philadelphia_hvi.csv`. You lose the local-file/automation angle but keep the analysis narrative.
- **No internet in the room:** pre-record the run, or screenshot each step into the slides as a backup.
- **Want a no-prompt, scripted version:** `claude -p "summarize data/philadelphia_hvi.csv"` runs once and exits --- useful to show automation, but do the interactive version first so students see the approval prompts.
- **Don't use `--dangerously-skip-permissions` in class** except to name it and explain why skipping approvals is risky.

### The three points to land

1. Agentic CLIs act on your **real files and tools**, not just chat text.
2. The **permission prompt is the safety boundary** --- you stay in the loop on every write and command.
3. Generation is cheap; **verification is the job.** Always check the numbers before the output leaves the room.
:::

:::

---

## Activity 3: Loops, Skills & Orchestration {.activity}

::: {.activity}

### Activity 3: Loops, Skills & Orchestration
::: {.activity-meta}
Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Hands-on exercise (individual or pairs)
:::

**Goal:** Build a reusable loop with explicit stop conditions, write a `SKILL.md` file, and design a multi-step orchestrated workflow.

:::{.callout-note}
## Background
"You are not building an agent. You are building the loop that rebuilds it." The durable asset is the loop --- the repeatable process of plan → execute → evaluate → iterate --- not the one-time output.
:::

#### Part A: Write a Loop (10 min)

A "loop" is a set of instructions that tells an agent what to do, how to check its work, and when to stop. Write a loop for one of these public health tasks (or choose your own):

**Option 1: Literature review sweep** --- Search for recent papers on your topic. For each: extract title, authors, year, key findings, relevance. VERIFY: check that each citation actually exists (search for the DOI). STOP: 15 verified papers or 50 search results exhausted.

**Option 2: Data quality audit** --- Load a dataset. Check each column for missing values, outliers, inconsistent formats, duplicates. VERIFY: for flagged issues, show specific rows for human confirmation. STOP: every column checked and all issues logged.

**Option 3: Grant section drafter** --- Read a research plan, draft a Significance section (500--800 words) with citations. VERIFY: check every factual claim against the cited source. Iterate to fix flagged issues. STOP: every claim sourced and section under 800 words.

Write your loop as a plain-text document with these sections: **GOAL**, **STEPS**, **VERIFY**, **STOP CONDITION**, **OUTPUT**.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: a complete loop (Data quality audit)

**GOAL:** Audit `philadelphia_hvi.csv` and produce a data-quality report a colleague can act on.

**STEPS:**

1. Load the file; print the row count and state the unit of analysis.
2. For each column: report type, % missing, min/max, and number of distinct values.
3. Flag: missing values, out-of-range values (e.g., `hvi_score` outside 0–4), duplicate tract IDs, and inconsistent formats.
4. For each flagged issue, pull 3–5 example rows.

**VERIFY:** For every flagged issue, show the specific rows to a human and wait for confirm/deny before recording it as a real problem. Do not auto-"fix" anything.

**STOP CONDITION:** Every column has been checked AND every flagged issue is confirmed or dismissed. Stop after one full pass — do not re-loop.

**OUTPUT:** `data_quality_report.md` — a one-line summary per column, a table of confirmed issues with example rows, and a "safe to analyze? yes/no" verdict.
:::

#### Part B: Write a SKILL.md (10 min)

A Skill is a reusable folder of expertise. Write a `SKILL.md` for a task you actually do at work:

```markdown
---
name: [short-kebab-case-name]
description: "[One sentence that tells the agent WHEN to load this skill]"
---

# [Skill Name]

## When to use
[Describe the trigger conditions]

## Instructions
[Step-by-step instructions the agent should follow]

## Output format
[What the deliverable should look like]

## Guardrails
[What the agent should NOT do]
```

*Ideas:* a de-identification checklist for data before sharing, a plain-language health communication rewriter, a STROBE/CONSORT reporting checklist, a data dictionary generator, a grant Specific Aims formatter.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: a finished SKILL.md (de-identification checklist)

````markdown
---
name: deidentify-before-sharing
description: "Use before sharing any dataset or document outside the team, to find identifiers that must be removed or masked."
---

# De-identification Checklist

## When to use
Whenever a user is about to export, email, or publish a dataset, table, or
document that may contain person-level information.

## Instructions
1. Scan for the 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers (names; geography smaller than
   state; all date elements except year; phone; email; MRN; etc.).
2. For each found: flag the field, show 2 example values, and propose an action
   (remove, generalize, or hash).
3. For dates, reduce to year unless the user confirms full dates are required
   and permitted.
4. For geography, output nothing finer than ZIP3 / census tract without
   explicit confirmation.
5. Produce a before/after summary of what changed.

## Output format
A table: field | identifier type | action taken | example before → after.
Plus a one-line "cleared for sharing: yes/no."

## Guardrails
- Never echo the original identifiers in the summary.
- Work on a copy; do not delete or overwrite the source file.
- If unsure whether something is an identifier, flag it for human review
  rather than passing it through.
````
:::

#### Part C: Design a Multi-Step Orchestration (10 min)

Design a workflow that uses multiple agents or steps. Example --- weekly surveillance report: Agent 1 (Data) pulls and cleans data → Agent 2 (Analysis) compares to previous weeks, generates charts → Agent 3 (Writing) drafts narrative report → Human checkpoint → Agent 4 (Distribution) formats and prepares email draft.

For your design, specify: (1) overall goal, (2) how many agents/steps, (3) what each does, (4) what is passed between steps, (5) where the human checkpoints are, (6) VERIFY and STOP conditions.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: a specified orchestration (weekly heat-surveillance report)

1. **Overall goal:** Every Monday, produce a one-page heat-illness surveillance update for the department director.
2. **Agents/steps:** four agents + one human checkpoint.
3. **What each does:**
   - *Data agent* — pull last week's ED heat-illness counts and NWS temperatures; clean and join by date.
   - *Analysis agent* — compare to the prior 4-week average and the same week last year; flag any tract above threshold; make one trend chart.
   - *Writing agent* — draft a plain-language narrative: what changed, where, and the recommended action.
   - **Human checkpoint** — an epidemiologist reviews the numbers and wording, then approves or edits.
   - *Distribution agent* — format the approved brief and prepare (not send) an email draft to the director.
4. **Passed between steps:** Data → a clean joined table; Analysis → table + chart + flags; Writing → draft markdown; Distribution → final brief + email draft.
5. **Human checkpoints:** one mandatory review before anything is formatted for sending. Nothing auto-sends.
6. **VERIFY / STOP:** Analysis re-checks each flagged tract's count against the raw table; STOP when the brief is one page and every number traces to the source table. If a week's data is missing, stop and alert the human instead of guessing.
:::

**Share Out:** 2--3 volunteers share their loop, skill, or orchestration design. What makes a good stop condition? What happens when the loop doesn't converge?

:::

---

## Activity 4: Local AI with Ollama {.activity}

::: {.activity}

### Activity 4: Local AI with Ollama
::: {.activity-meta}
Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Demonstration + optional hands-on installation
:::

**Goal:** Understand why local AI models matter for sensitive data and see how to run them.

#### Why Local Models?

When you use ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, your prompts and data are sent to external servers. For many public health tasks, this is fine. But for work involving:

- **Protected Health Information (PHI)**
- **Personally identifiable data**
- **Pre-publication research data**
- **Sensitive agency communications**
- **Data covered by IRB protocols**

...you may need AI that runs entirely on your own machine, with **no data leaving your computer**.

#### What is Ollama?

[Ollama](https://ollama.com) is a free, open-source tool that lets you run AI models locally on your laptop. It supports many models, including:

- **Llama 3.x / Llama 4** (Meta) --- good general-purpose models
- **Gemma 4** (Google) --- lightweight and capable; runs well on a laptop
- **Qwen / DeepSeek** --- strong open models (note: developed in China; data stays local when run via Ollama)
- **Mistral** --- fast and efficient
- **Phi** (Microsoft) --- optimized for smaller hardware

#### Installation (for those who can install software)

```bash
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install ollama

# Or download from https://ollama.com/download

# Start Ollama
ollama serve

# Pull and run a model (pick one that fits your laptop)
ollama run qwen3          # strong default; try qwen3:4b on 8 GB laptops
ollama run gemma4         # Google's efficient option
```

**System Requirements:**

- At least 8 GB RAM (16 GB recommended)
- 5--10 GB free disk space per model
- macOS, Linux, or Windows

#### Browser-Based Alternatives (for restricted computers)

If you cannot install software on your work computer:

- **[Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com):** Free, browser-based, runs Gemini models with reasonable privacy policies
- **[HuggingChat](https://huggingface.co/chat/):** Free, open-source models, browser-based
- **Note:** These still send data to external servers but may have stronger privacy protections than consumer products

#### Demo

The instructor will demonstrate:

1. Installing and running Ollama
2. Chatting with a local model
3. Comparing response quality to cloud-based models (trade-offs: speed, quality, privacy)
4. When local models are “good enough” vs. when you need cloud models

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Worked example: a 5-minute local-vs-cloud comparison

A concrete task to run once Ollama is installed. Use **synthetic** text only — never real PHI in a classroom.

**1. Start a local model:**

```bash
ollama run qwen3
```

**2. Paste this de-identification task (synthetic note):**

> Rewrite the following clinic note to remove all HIPAA identifiers, replacing each with a bracketed tag like `[NAME]` or `[DATE]`. Return only the de-identified note.
>
> "Maria Gonzalez (DOB 03/14/1958), MRN 4471209, was seen on June 2, 2026 at Health Center 4 on W Allegheny Ave for heat exhaustion. Call back at 215-555-0142."

**3. Run the *same* prompt in a cloud tool** — [Claude.ai](https://claude.ai) or [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com).

**4. Compare on three axes:**

- **Quality:** Did each catch every identifier (name, DOB, MRN, date, address, phone)? Local models sometimes miss one — note which.
- **Speed:** Time each response.
- **Privacy:** With Ollama the note never left your laptop; with the cloud tool it was sent to a server — which is decisive if this were real PHI.

**Takeaway:** for a sensitive, well-scoped task like de-identification, a local model that's “good enough” can be the right call *because* the data stays put. For open-ended drafting where quality matters most and the data isn't sensitive, cloud models usually win.
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## Advanced companion: agentic workflows on a local model (notebook)

Want to go further than chatting? This [Jupyter notebook](code/Agentic_Workflows_UrbanHealth_2026.ipynb){download="Agentic_Workflows_UrbanHealth_2026.ipynb"} builds real **agentic workflows** on a local Ollama model (a laptop-friendly model like Gemma 4 or Qwen3) — everything runs on your machine, free and private. You pick an everyday triage task — **311 complaints, food-safety violations, or pedestrian-injury surveillance** — and work it through three patterns:

1. **One structured-output prompt** that triages one report against your rule (Pydantic + JSON mode).
2. **A multi-agent crew** (CrewAI) — a triage agent and a documentation agent collaborating.
3. **A state machine** (LangGraph) — the same workflow as an explicit graph with a conditional branch.

Plus a tool-calling agent (a keyword search + a triage counter over your queue) that previews what MCP standardizes, and a "try it yourself" exercise. It ties directly to the Day 3 concepts: the agent loop, context engineering, tools/MCP, guardrails, and verification.

Run it in **Positron**, VS Code, or Jupyter. Install once with `pip install ollama crewai langgraph langchain-ollama pydantic`. Best as an optional advanced track or a live instructor demo.
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## For IT-Restricted Computers
Many government and health department computers restrict software installation. If you cannot install Ollama on your work computer, the browser-based alternatives above provide a middle ground. For truly sensitive work, talk to your IT department about setting up a local AI environment on a secure server.
:::

:::

---

::: {.bonus-challenges-section}

## Bonus Challenge Exercises

These optional exercises are for students who finish the core activities early or want to go deeper. See the full [Bonus Challenges page](challenges.qmd) for complete instructions and all 13 exercises across the course.

::: {.challenge-card}

#### Challenge 6: Building an Automated Data Pipeline

::: {.challenge-meta}
**Difficulty:** Expert | **Time:** 90 min | **Tools:** Claude Code, Python, OpenDataPhilly API
:::

Build a Python pipeline with Claude Code that acquires 311 service request data from the OpenDataPhilly API, cleans and geocodes it, computes per-tract complaint rates and trends, and generates an HTML report with charts and narrative. Document architecture and error handling.

[Full instructions →](challenges.qmd#exercise-6)

:::

::: {.challenge-card}

#### Challenge 7: Custom MCP Server Design

::: {.challenge-meta}
**Difficulty:** Expert | **Time:** 60 min | **Tools:** Any text editor or word processor
:::

Choose a public health scenario (disease surveillance, community health assessment, or environmental inspection support). Design a complete MCP server specification with 6--10 tools, security constraints, a 500--800 word system prompt, and a capability comparison table.

[Full instructions →](challenges.qmd#exercise-7)

:::

::: {.challenge-card}

#### Challenge 8: Local vs. Cloud Model Evaluation

::: {.challenge-meta}
**Difficulty:** Intermediate | **Time:** 45 min | **Tools:** Ollama (Gemma 4 or Qwen3), Claude or ChatGPT
:::

Create 10 test prompts across five categories (factual knowledge, data interpretation, writing, reasoning, code generation). Run them through both a local Ollama model and a cloud model, score each response, and write a recommendation memo for your department on when to use each.

[Full instructions →](challenges.qmd#exercise-8)

:::

::: {.challenge-card}

#### Challenge 9: AI-Assisted Grant Proposal Section

::: {.challenge-meta}
**Difficulty:** Advanced | **Time:** 75 min | **Tools:** Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), Perplexity
:::

Choose a research topic at the intersection of AI and urban health. Use iterative AI-assisted drafting to produce a 1-page NIH-format Specific Aims section and a 2-page Significance/Innovation section, tracking which content was AI-generated vs. human-written at each stage.

[Full instructions →](challenges.qmd#exercise-9)

:::

:::

---

## Today's Readings

See the [Readings page](readings.qmd#day-3-readings) for today's assigned and recommended readings.

---

## Looking Ahead

Tomorrow we shift from **tools** to **workflows**. You will build a simple agent in Google AI Studio, assess your own work processes for AI augmentation potential, work through realistic urban health scenarios in small groups, and explore AI-assisted grant writing. Come prepared to think critically about your daily work and where AI fits in.
